# Figure description: скв_150.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/скв_150.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a Soviet-era geophysical well log strip chart. It appears to be a multi-curve wireline log displayed on a long vertical strip, typical of Soviet exploratory drilling documentation. The figure also includes **tabular data columns** on both the left and right margins, which likely contain lithological descriptions, core recovery data, and geophysical parameter values by depth interval.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a single borehole or well (скважина), most likely one of the exploratory wells drilled as part of the regional survey under Report 36572. The log runs vertically over what appears to be a **substantial depth range** — based on the length of the strip and Soviet standard log scales, likely on the order of **several hundred to possibly 1,000+ metres**. The header area (top of the sheet) contains identifying information including what appears to be a borehole number, location coordinates or map sheet reference, and survey parameters, though most of this text is **[illegible due to scan quality]**. A **separate smaller panel** at the bottom of the sheet shows an additional log segment or a summary/repeat section.

---

## Key Content

### Log Curves
The central portion of the figure displays **two or three continuous geophysical curves** running the full depth of the borehole:

- **Left curve (narrower amplitude):** Likely a **spontaneous potential (СП / SP)** or **gamma-ray (ГК / GK)** log, showing relatively smooth but moderate deflections throughout.
- **Right/central curve (wider, more energetic deflections):** Likely an **electrical resistivity (КС / KS)** or **apparent resistivity** curve, showing sharp high-amplitude peaks and troughs. This curve dominates the visual character of the log and shows:
  - **High-amplitude, sharp spikes** in the upper and lower thirds of the log, suggesting zones of resistive (possibly carbonate, evaporite, or crystalline basement) lithologies.
  - **Subdued, lower-amplitude sections** in the middle portion, consistent with more conductive (clay-rich, water-saturated, or fractured) intervals.
  - A **particularly prominent anomalous zone** approximately one-quarter to one-third from the top, where the curve deflects maximally — possibly indicating a significant lithological boundary or mineralised interval.

### Marginal Tables
- **Left margin:** Contains a **lithological/stratigraphic column** with formation names, depth intervals, core descriptions, and possibly drilling parameters. Multiple row entries are visible but text is largely **[illegible]**.
- **Right margin:** Contains a **multi-column numerical table** with depth intervals and corresponding values — likely summarising geophysical readings (e.g., resistivity values, porosity estimates, or formation evaluation parameters) at discrete depth intervals.

### Lower Panel
A **separate shorter log panel** at the very bottom appears to show a repeat or check section, or possibly a different logging run (e.g., a caliper or temperature log), with smoother curve character.

## Research Value

This composite well log is the **primary subsurface data source** for characterising lithological variation and formation boundaries along the borehole depth profile, providing direct evidence of the physical properties of rocks encountered during drilling in this part of northern Kazakhstan. A geologist or AI research assistant would use this to **identify resistivity anomalies potentially indicative of mineralised zones, aquifers, evaporites, or basement contacts**, and to correlate subsurface stratigraphy with surface mapping and seismic/gravity data from the same survey. In the context of mineral exploration, the high-amplitude resistivity peaks are of particular interest as they may delineate economically significant horizons (e.g., sulphide mineralisation, salt layers, or crystalline basement highs) warranting further investigation.
